Individual Project, DSCI 100 (80 points)¶

INSTRUCTIONS¶

Complete the following questions individually using the provided data set. When prompted, fill out the cells with your code (in code cells) or text (in text cells) to answer the question. The cells will have "%%% EDIT THIS MARKDOWN CELL %%%" or "### EDIT THIS CODE CELL ###" prompting you to overwrite the contents of the cell with your answer.

Your report must be fully reproducible, and run without error by itself with no other files.

REFERENCES¶

Students are required to use methods discussed in DSCI 100 for the project report. However, there may be some stages where you wish to research on how to handle "messy" data, including outliers, missing data, etc. If you refer to anything beyond DSCI 100, please include a link to the reference at the end of this report. It does not need to be a "formal" reference like an academic paper or website, you can link to blog posts or websites like StackExchange if they are relevant to your analysis.

GENERATIVE AI USE¶

This analysis is based on methods learned in DSCI 100, so you should have all of the tools you need to write this code from scratch. You may use Generative AI (i.e., ChatGPT, Gemini, etc) as an "advanced search engine" (i.e., to help de-bug code).You cannot use it to generate code or text on the report. Any use of Generative AI must be disclosed at the bottom of this project report.

An example of a disclosure of appropriate Gen AI use: "I tried to fit a model using function X and got error Y. Gen AI helped me figure out that the error was due to a missing value."

Failure to disclose Generative AI use is a form of academic misconduct. Submissions suspected of inappropriately using Gen AI will be flagged by the teaching team and handled on a case-by-case basis.

TO GET STARTED¶

  • "Accept the Assignment" using the link on the Canvas "Project: 2026" description. This will create a GitHub repository in our DSCI 100 Classroom Organization. 
  • Clone the GitHub repository from your GitHub Classroom Assignment into your Jupyter Lab so that you can access it on Jupyter (see YouTube tutorial: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUgOZaRddSQ).
  • Copy the link to your repository into the project report (see Task 0.1)!

DUE DATES¶

To keep you on track, there are a number of checkpoints in which we require some work to be uploaded to your GitHub repository (drafts of your work!). They are as follows:

  • Checkpoint A (up to and including Task 0.1): Saturday February 7th, 11:59pm
  • Checkpoint B (up to and including Task 3.1): Saturday March 14th, 11:59pm
  • Checkpoint C (up to and including Task 5.1): Saturday March 21st, 11:59pm
  • Final Submission: March 28th, 11:59pm

All dates and times listed are local time in Vancouver, BC (PT)

Part 0: Set Up (1 point)¶

In this section, you will get your GitHub repository set up for the project.

  • Copy the link to your repository in the cell below. It does not need to be public.

  • Set your seed as your student number in the code cell below your name and GitHub Repository link.

CHECKPOINT A (1 point)¶

At this point, save, stage, commit, and push your notebook to your GitHub repository by Saturday February 7th, 2026, 11:59pm PT.

IN YOUR COMMIT MESSAGE/TITLE, WRITE "CHECKPOINT A".

This is a draft. We require a commit of your work at this stage, but it does not need to be your final product. You can edit the document later.

Part 1: The Data (2 points)¶

We will be exploring a data set on Women's March Madness (Basketball). This adapted data set contains historical records of every NCAA Division I Women’s Basketball Tournament appearance since the tournament began in 1982 up until 2018. Sourced from official NCAA records, the adapted data set captures tournament results across more than four decades of collegiate women’s basketball. All data is sourced from the NCAA and contains the data behind the story "The Rise and Fall Of Women’s NCAA Tournament Dynasties."

The data set can be read in from the following url: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/diverse-data-hub/diversedata/refs/heads/main/data-clean/womensmarchmadness.csv

To help you with the analysis, here is a table of descriptions of each of the variables present in the model.

Variable Description
year Year
school School Name
seed Seed. The 0 seeding designation in 1983 notes the eight teams that played an opening-round game to become the No. 8 seed in each region.
conference Conference
conf_wins Conference Wins
conf_losses Conference Losses
conf_wins_pct Conference Win Percentage
conf_rank Place in Conference
division Conference Division
reg_wins Regional Wins
reg_losses Regional Losses
reg_wins_pct Regional Win Percentage
bid Whether the school qualified with an automatic bid (by winning its conference or conference tournament) or an at-large bid. [‘at-large’, ‘auto’]
first_game_at_home Whether the school played its first-round tournament games on its home court. [‘Y’, ‘N’]
tourney_wins Tourney Wins
tourney_losses Tourney Losses
tourney_finish Ordered categories: [‘opening_round_loss’ < ‘first_round_loss’ < ‘second_round_loss’ < ‘top_16_loss’ < ‘top_8_loss’ < ‘top_4_loss’ < ‘top_2_loss’ < ‘champ’]
total_wins Total Wins
total_losses Total Losses
total_wins_pct Total Win Percentage

Task 1.1: Read in the Data (2 points)¶

  • Use your knowledge of R to load in the data from the url given.
  • Print out the first 6 rows to view the structure of the data.
In [2]:
# Loads the tidyverse and tidymodels libraries 
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)

# Note: The data can also be read with read.csv (With a period) in base R
basketball_data <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/diverse-data-hub/diversedata/refs/heads/main/data-clean/womensmarchmadness.csv")

# Show the first 6 rows 
head(basketball_data, 6)

Part 2: Research Questions (4 points)¶

You will think about a predictive research question that's relevant to your data set!

Task 2.1: Write Your Research Question (2 points)¶

  • From your initial view of the data set, and given the available data in the data sets, write a predictive research question that you may be able to explore. You response should be a single sentence, and phrased as a question.

For example, if I had data on flower species, petal length, and petal widths, I may wonder... "can petal width and length be used to predict the species of flower?"

Can I use a team's seed, bid, and various metrics of win percentages (Such as regional win rate, conference win rate, and total win rate) to predict the tournament finish?

Task 2.2: Identifying Research Question Type (2 points)¶

Write your answer to the following question in the cell below.

  • Is your research question a classification or regression-type predictive question? Why?

Your answer should be 50 words or less (1 sentence)

This is a classification problem, because we use the predictor variables to create a prediction, the tournament finish of a given team.

Part 3: Tidying (5 points)¶

You will complete specific tasks for the chosen data set

Task 3.1: Tidy the data (5 points)¶

Produce a tidy data set that could be used to answer your research question using the tidyverse package. Please ensure you have at least one quantitative (numeric) and one categorical variable:

  • Create new variables (if needed)
  • Transform the data into a tidy form (if needed)
  • Remove irrelevant columns (if needed)
  • Comment your code throughout
  • Show the first 6 rows of the tidied data.
In [3]:
# Selected the original dataset with the relevant variables:
# Seed, regional win percentage, conference win percentage, bid, tournament finish, and tournament wins and losses, total win percentage
tidy_basketball_data <- select(basketball_data, seed, reg_wins_pct, conf_wins_pct, bid, tourney_wins, tourney_losses, tourney_finish, total_wins_pct) 

# I am also going to create a new variable representing the tournament win rate 
tidy_basketball_data <- tidy_basketball_data |>
mutate(tourney_wins_pct = tourney_wins / (tourney_wins + tourney_losses))

# Later in the project, I realized that there are extremely little observations of opening round loss, which lead to some errors 
# I am going to group it with the next lowest category, first_round_loss, into one variable called opening_or_first
# This way, I can get a decent total number of observations 
tidy_basketball_data <- tidy_basketball_data |>
mutate(tourney_finish = ifelse(tourney_finish == "opening_round_loss", "opening_or_first", tourney_finish)) |>
mutate(tourney_finish = ifelse(tourney_finish == "first_round_loss", "opening_or_first", tourney_finish))

# I will turn bid and tourney_finish into a factor 
tidy_basketball_data <- tidy_basketball_data |>
mutate(bid = as_factor(bid)) |>
mutate(tourney_finish = as_factor(tourney_finish))

# I do not need the tourney_wins and losses directly. I only needed it to calculate the win rate. 
# Since I do not need these columns anymore, I will remove them from the dataframe. 
tidy_basketball_data <- tidy_basketball_data |>
select(- tourney_wins) |>
select(- tourney_losses)

# Show the first 6 rows 
head(tidy_basketball_data, 6)

Part 4: Summarizing and Exploring (18 points)¶

In this section, you will explore the data and summarize some relationships.

Task 4.1: Calculate summary statistics of numeric variables (2 points)¶

  • Use any functions from the tidyverse to create one table that outputs the mean, minimum, and maximum of all numeric columns in your data, dropping the missing values if they exist.
  • Show the outputted table.
In [5]:
# There are missing values, so I used na.rm = TRUE to drop them. We are going to calculate the amount of missingness later on
# I choose seed as the variable we are going to calculate the mean of 
numeric_summary <- tidy_basketball_data |>
group_by(tourney_finish) |>
summarise(mean = mean(seed, na.rm = TRUE),
         min = min(seed, na.rm = TRUE), 
         max = max(seed, na.rm = TRUE))

numeric_summary

  • Show the outputted table.

# The "bid" field has 61 missing values but the rest have none 
missing_values <- colSums(is.na(tidy_basketball_data))
missing_values <- data.frame(missing_values)
missing_values

Task 4.3: Summarize over levels of a categorical variable (2 points)¶

  • Use your tidyverse knowledge to create one table that calculates mean of one numeric variable for each of the levels of another categorical variable.
  • Show the outputted table.
In [6]:
# Making sure I understand this correctly:
# Pick one of my numeric variables 
# For each of the categories of tourney_finish, calculate the mean value of all the rows
# With the given category, using the numeric variable I chose to calculate the mean 

# First, I will remove all the rows with missing values 
tidy_basketball_data <- tidy_basketball_data |> 
drop_na(seed, reg_wins_pct, conf_wins_pct, bid, tourney_finish)

# I will choose seed as my numeric variable
mean_values <- tidy_basketball_data |>
  group_by(tourney_finish) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(seed))

mean_values

Task 4.4: Plot the distribution of two relevant variables in the raw data (4 points)¶

  • Use ggplot to plot the distribution of one of the numeric variables in your tidied data set.

  • Use ggplot to create a visualization showing the relative proportions of each category/level for one of categorical variable in your tidied data set.

  • Clearly label the variable that is being plotted in each plot.

Notes:

  • Be sure the output is as clear as possible, provide an English title along with readable x and y column names

  • You may also add more code cells to this section

In [7]:
# I will plot the distribution of the regional wins percentage, reg_wins_pct
basketball_plot_1 <- ggplot(tidy_basketball_data, aes(x = reg_wins_pct)) +
geom_histogram() + 
labs(x = "Regional Wins Perctange Range", y = "Frequency", title = "Distribution of Regional Win Percentages")

basketball_plot_1

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`.
In [7]:
# I will plot the distribution of the tournament finishes
# I made a horizontal bar plot to prevent the category names from overlapping
basketball_plot_2 <- ggplot(tidy_basketball_data, aes(y = tourney_finish)) +
geom_bar() + 
labs(y = "Tournament Finish", title = "Relative Proportions of Tournament Finishes")

basketball_plot_2

Part 4.5: Visualize Relationships (8 points)¶

Based on your research question:

  • Create at least two visualizations that look at the relationship between two variables that are relevant to the research question.
  • For each visualization, briefly describe how this is relevant to the analysis (in 100 words or less).
  • Be sure the plots are clear, concise, and readable. Provide English titles and axis labels.

You may add more markdown and code cells if you choose to provide more than two plots.

FIRST PLOT

A scatter plot of the regional win percentage against the conference wins percentage. It is important to know how strong the correlation is between success at the regional and conference level, if a correlation exists at all. An argument against there being a strong correlation might be that there is a disparity in a team’s skill level relative to the surrounding competitors, and the region. They may perform worse in conference but better overall due to competing against especially strong teams during the conference, or vice versa due to facing weaker teams. However, we see a relatively strong correlation (r = 0.7769).

In [8]:
reg_vs_conf_wins_pct <- ggplot(tidy_basketball_data, aes(x = conf_wins_pct, y = reg_wins_pct)) + 
geom_point() + 
labs(x = "Conference Win Percentage", y = "Regional Wins Percentage", title = "Corellation Between Regional and Conference Win Percentages")

reg_vs_conf_wins_pct

Warning message:
“Removed 61 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).”

SECOND PLOT

A scatter plot of the tournament win percentage against the seed. There should be a decreasing linear relationship because: A lower seed represents a higher rank, or in other words, a stronger team. Of course, a stronger team theoretically should result in a higher total win percentage. This correlation is not perfect, but I would like to see how strong the relationship is (r = -0.5105119, and as we can see this relationship is moderate to weak). This visualization and the one before it are both important since it shows that there are underlying mechanisms that relate different measures of performance to one another.

In [10]:
total_wins_pct_vs_seed <- ggplot(tidy_basketball_data, aes(x = seed, y = total_wins_pct)) +
geom_point() + 
labs(x = "Tournament Seed", y = "Total Win Percentages", title = "Correlation Between Overall Win Percentage and Tournament Speed")

total_wins_pct_vs_seed

Part 5: Analysis (34 points)¶

In this section, you will describe your data analysis and perform it.

Task 5.1 Plan out your analysis (10 points)¶

Propose a method that was covered in class that would be appropriate to use to answer your research question. You only need to build one model with one set of predictors in this section. Write a paragraph that explains the choice of method, and be sure to answer the following questions:

  • What method do you plan to use?
  • Why is this method appropriate?
  • What are the potential limitations or weaknesses of the method selected?
  • How are you going to process the data to apply the model? For example: Are you splitting the data? How? How many splits? What proportions will you use for the splits? At what stage will you split? Will there be a validation set? Will you use cross validation?
  • How will you assess the performance of the model? Explain why the performance metric(s) chosen are appropriate.

You answer should be around 250 words or less.

For my analysis, I plan to use a K Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model to predict a team’s tournament win percentage using the predictors: Seed, regional win percentage, conference win percentage, total win percentage, tournament wins percentages, and bid. KNN is appropriate because it performs well regardless of the shape of my data, or the relationships inside of it. Since KNN relies on distance, I can scale and center all my predictors to have an equal influence on my prediction.

Examples of limitations include: Performance may decrease when many predictors are included, and the model cannot extrapolate beyond the range of the training data. This second limitation is not a major concern here because most predictors are percentages bounded between 0 and 100. To apply the model, I will immediately split the dataset into 75% training and 25% testing before fitting. Within the training set, I will use 5 fold cross validation to select the optimal value of K.

Since this analysis uses random sampling, I will use a for-loop to run this analysis several times in a row. Then, I will gather the values of K and choose the mode. Theoretically, this approach may still cause me to choose different values of K, but my choice will be more justified. The model's performance will be assessed by its success rate. This is appropriate because we are not predicting a number (As then we would use RMSE), so the rate of successful predictions is a better measure of its accuracy.

CHECKPOINT C (1 point)¶

At this point, save, stage, commit, and push your notebook to your GitHub repository by Saturday March 21st 2026, 11:59pm PT.

IN YOUR COMMIT MESSAGE/TITLE, WRITE "CHECKPOINT C".

This is a draft. We require a commit at this stage, but it does not need to be your final product. You can edit the document later.

Task 5.2 Perform the Analysis (20 points)¶

  • Perform the proposed analysis.
  • Be sure to comment your code throughout so that it is easy to follow.

You may add more code cells if needed.

In [12]:
# We will need to load this library
library(recipes)

# Create a new dataframe - I will the analysis 50 times 
k_values <- data.frame(matrix(ncol = 1, nrow = 0))
colnames(k_values) <- c("K")

# Run a for loop to conduct the analysis 50 times 
for (x in 1:50) {
    # First, split the data into the training and testing split 
    # Make sure to use stratified sampling on the tournament finish variable 
    basketball_split <- initial_split(tidy_basketball_data, prop = 0.75, strata = tourney_finish)
    basketball_training <- training(basketball_split)
    basketball_testing <- testing(basketball_split)

    # We will use one-hot encoding to use bid as a predictor variable 
    basketball_recipe <- recipe(tourney_finish ~ seed + reg_wins_pct + conf_wins_pct +  
                                tourney_wins_pct + bid + total_wins_pct, data = basketball_training) |>
    step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot = TRUE) |>
    step_novel(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
    step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
    step_scale(all_predictors()) |>
    step_center(all_predictors()) 

    # Create the KNN model 
    basketball_spec <- nearest_neighbor(weight_func = "rectangular", neighbors = tune()) |>
    set_engine("kknn") |>
    set_mode("classification")

    # Create the workflow 
    basketball_workflow <- workflow() |>
    add_recipe(basketball_recipe) |>
    add_model(basketball_spec) |> 
    fit(data = basketball_training)

    # Create the vfold 
    basketball_vfold <- vfold_cv(basketball_training, v = 5, strata = tourney_finish)

    # I will have to decide on choosing the optimal value of K, from 1 to 10 neighbors 
    gridvals <- tibble(neighbors = seq(from = 1, to = 10, by = 1))

    # Perform the tuning 
    basketball_results <- basketball_workflow |>
    tune_grid(resamples = basketball_vfold, grid = gridvals) |>
    collect_metrics() |>
    filter(.metric == "accuracy")

    # Fetch the value giving the highest accuracy 
    optimal_k_value <- basketball_results |>
    arrange(desc(mean)) |>
    select(neighbors) |>
    head(1) |>
    pull()

    # Create a row representign the optimal value of K created from this iteration of the analysis 
    this_row <- tibble(K = c(optimal_k_value))

    # Add it to the current dataframe 
    k_values <- bind_rows(k_values, this_row)
}

In [13]:
# Now, we are going to complete the analysis by selecting the mode 
k_value <- k_values |>
table() |>
which.max() |>
names() |>
as.numeric()

# Now, we will perform the analysis after having determined our K value 
basketball_spec <- nearest_neighbor(weight_func = "rectangular", neighbors = k_value) |>
set_engine("kknn") |>
set_mode("classification")

# Remake the workflow with the new model 
basketball_fit <- workflow() |>
add_recipe(basketball_recipe) |>
add_model(basketball_spec) |>
fit(data = basketball_training)

# Test against testing set 
basketball_summary <- basketball_fit |>
predict(basketball_testing) |> 
bind_cols(basketball_testing) |>
metrics(truth = tourney_finish, estimate = .pred_class) |>
filter(.metric == "accuracy")

k_value
basketball_summary
3
A tibble: 1 × 3
.metric.estimator.estimate
<chr><chr><dbl>
accuracymulticlass0.8349705

Task 5.3 Create a Visualization or Table of the Results of your Analysis (4 points)¶

  • Create a visualization or table that is relevant to the results of your analysis (comment your code throughout so that it is easy to follow).
  • Describe what this visualization or table is showing, and why it is important to the analysis (approx 150 words or less).

This visualization is a histogram showing the different values of K that were recorded during each iteration of my analysis. Since my analysis involves random sampling, an issue that I did not foresee was that I received inconsistent values when I used 5-fold cross validation to determine what choice of K would give me the highest accuracy amongst the training set. Hence, I wrapped my code around a for loop to conduct the analysis several times. I then created a new data frame outside of the for loop and added rows to it during each iteration of my analysis. To test the accuracy of the model, my final choice of K was the mode of the different K-values that I had derived from running the for loop. This visualization shows the distribution of the various values I had derived from running the analysis 50 times in a row.

In [14]:
# Plotting the distribution of the different K values that I collected 
k_values_plot <- ggplot(k_values, aes(x = K)) + 
geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.75) + 
labs(x = "K (Number of Neighbors)") + 
ggtitle("Distribution of K Collected from Various Iterations of Analysis")

k_values_plot

Part 6: Discuss your Results (10 points)¶

In this section, you will discuss some interesting findings from your analysis in Part 5

Task 6.1: Discuss your findings (10 points)¶

In 1 to 2 paragraphs (approx. 300 words or less):

  • Summarize what you found from the analysis in Part 5, in relation to your research question of interest.
  • The data science workflow is an iterative process. Discuss if at any point you had to adjust your analysis plan based on the results.
  • Discuss whether this is what you expected, or if anything was surprising.
  • Discuss the impact of the results, and what future questions this could lead to.

From my analysis in part 5, my answer to my research question is: Yes, I can predict the tournament finish based on the given data and the predictors I have chosen, with an accuracy rate consistently above 80%. While testing the model, I encountered several situations that required me to adjust my plans. For example, I received an error telling me that there was a category of tourney_finish (The variable I was predicting) that went unseen, namely "opening_round_loss". This issue occurred because there were certain iterations in which the training split contained an observation with this category and the testing split did not, or vice versa. To solve this issue, I grouped this category with “first_round_loss,” the next lowest category in the hierarchy. Hence, I created a new variable called "opening_or_first", to replace them both. This new variable had enough total observations to be used for stratified sampling.

Prior to completing the project, I did not know what accuracy level to expect. After the first few iterations of my analysis, I modified my plan further, because I would get inconsistent values for K because it relies on random sampling. It was only then that I realized I should wrap my analysis around a for-loop and repeat it 50 times consecutively with the training data, accumulating the K-values from each iteration. I then took the mode of the K values, effectively finding me the value that consistently produces the best results. The analysis shows that the tournament finish is very much predictable, which means that it is possible to identify over-performance or under-performance based on the certain predictors chosen. Some future questions I have are: Can another model outperform KNN? Also, is there an underlying mechanism behind the chosen predictors to create accurate predictions?

REFERENCES¶

If you used any resources beyond the DSCI 100 content, please link them below. Failure to provide references is a form of academic misconduct.

Finding the number of mising values in each column:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/24027605/determine-the-number-of-na-values-in-a-column

Learning to use step_dummy for one hot encoding:
https://recipes.tidymodels.org/reference/step_dummy.html
https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/recipes/versions/1.2.1/topics/step_dummy
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71239648/tidymodels-recipes-can-i-use-step-dummy-to-one-hot-encode-the-categorical-var

Finding the mode:
https://r-coder.com/which-max-min-r/ https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/base/versions/3.6.2/topics/table
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2547402/how-to-find-the-statistical-mode
https://dev.to/rapp2043/finding-the-mode-in-r-a-step-by-step-guide-49h1
https://www.statology.org/mode-in-r/

DISCLOSURE OF GENERATIVE AI USE¶

If you used Generative AI for any part of this project, disclose it below.

An example of appropriate Generative AI disclosure: If you used Gen AI to debug the error, you can disclose: "I tried to fit a model using X function and got error Y. Gen AI helped me figure out that the error was due to a missing value."

Failure to disclose Generative AI use is a form of academic misconduct.

When I tried to user one-hot encoding for my analysis, I received an error for trying to manually pass in the categorical variables I needed to use. ChatGPT helped me figure out an alternative, which was to use all_nominal_predictors() as an argument instead.

SUBMISSION (3 points)¶

Review your report and prepare it for the final draft. Save, stage, commit, and push the changes to your GitHub repository. You can do this as many times as needed prior to the deadline (i.e., you can have more than 5 commits). (1 point)

To prepare for your submission, your GitHub Repository must contain:

  • This report saved as a .html file (File -> Download As -> HTML)
  • The Jupyter Notebook (.ipynb) file. This file should run from top-to-bottom with no errors, and be completely reproducible. Note: some plots may appear distorted when viewing on GitHub
  • A minimum of 4 substantial commits (3 checkpoints + final submission)

PLEASE SUBMIT THE FOLLOWING TO CANVAS BY MARCH 28th 11:59pm

  • The .html file of your report, which includes a link to the GitHub Repository in your report (1 point)
  • The .ipynb file of your report, which includes a link to the GitHub Repository in your report (1 point)
